speaker: interviewee
text: I think before we want to explore the possibility of immigration, I think I should go back to the beginning of 1938. In the beginning of 1938, I got sick. At one point, it was suspected that I had measles, which I did. But then I also got a cough and they decided that it was a mild case of whooping cough. My parents were very concerned about this and lung damage is always a problem with this, especially in those days when there were no antibiotics. And it lasted for about a month. And after that, my doctor decided that I should go to Switzerland with my mother for an extended period and take salt baths there. So the papers were requested and finally in August, we were able to leave. And we were supposed to be gone for three months and go to three different places. The first place we went to was Davos, where we stayed at San Souci Hotel, which is no longer there. And we-- I took the baths every day and we walked around the area and we had a wonderful vacation. When we got our passports, we also got my father's passport and he promised that he would come. 1938, in the summer was already a very difficult time. Austria had fallen to the Germans and Hitler was making all kinds of noises. And my mother kept calling home and my father kept saying, I can't leave the business, I can't leave the business. This was the time when they were trying to sell the business. After a month, we went from Davos to [? Lugano ?] and stayed at a hotel there, also Sans Souci. And I took the baths there. And about then, Yugoslavia-- I mean Czechoslovakia-- fell to the Germans and my mother got more and more irritated because my father wasn't there. And she kept calling and he kept saying, soon, soon, soon. And we stayed there until the beginning of November, late October. The Polish Jews that lived in Germany were rounded up and deported to Poland and my mother was very restless, very concerned about my father. And she insisted that even though we had another month on our papers, she was going back because he wasn't coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Business. Business was always the problem. And the other thing was a mental set, I am a veteran of the German first war and nothing is going to happen to me. And this is marked on his grave, regrettably. We got back to Speyer by train about the 3rd or 4th of November. And of course, on the 6th of November came Kristallnacht. And that morning real early, about five o'clock in the morning, there was a lot of commotion on the street. My parents woke us-- woke me-- and we found out that the synagogue was on fire and that the Nazis had smashed the windows on all the stores that were still owned by Germans. We stayed in our house, didn't dare go out, and found out later from people who were loyal to us, who had worked for us and who were still working for the new owners but told us things, that people tried to put the fire out but no one was permitted to go near the synagogue. And even the fire department was not allowed to go near. And later, we found out that the SS had smashed the doors and took all the valuables out of the temple-- not the [? torahs, ?] but everything else-- and then pull-- pulled two wagons of hay into the synagogue and set them on fire with gasoline. And so that was that. Later that day, they came and arrested my father. And they told us at the time that he would be in the local jail and that my mother should bring food to him-- the-- the meals. So at noon or in the afternoon, she took a meal to him and then we were told we had to leave town immediately. At the time, my mother's younger sister who had married in 1932 and her husband were visiting, which no one knew. He could drive. My father was never a driver, but he wasn't available anyway. No one else knew how to drive. So the decision was made that he would drive us in one of the business cars, which we still owned, and take us to our-- my mother's oldest sister in Stuttgart. So late at night-- the expression is 'en nacht und nabel,' in night and fog, we packed up what we could, my grandmother, my mother and I, and my aunt and uncle, and we drove to Stuttgart. It was a very foggy night and going along the Rhine River on the autostrasse-- it wasn't an autobahn yet at that time, it was very difficult. But we got there early in the morning and the reception was less than cordial. She was worried about her husband who was also on a business trip. He was also a salesman and he was caught in a small town and she didn't know whether he was OK or not. But anyway, we moved in. And it got tighter and tighter as far as being able to escape. And finally, my uncle and aunt decided they were going to go on to Frankfurt, where he was more familiar with the area and had friends that could possibly hide him. Well, he got to Frankfurt and the first thing he did was run into a Nazi barricade and he was arrested and the car was taken into custody. My aunt stayed with some friends there. We later find out that Uncle Heinz, who was the uncle from Stuttgart-- his wife's name, my mother's older sister, was Elsa, and their son, Martin-- that Heinz had been arrested in this small town. He never went to Dachau, but he was regularly bludgeoned in this small jail in this small town by jailers or whoever wanted to have a go at him. And he would have probably been better off in Dachau than he was there. My Uncle Ernst, who also lived in Speyer with his wife, Ruth, and their daughter, Liesel, he also wound up in Dachau. Ruth and Liesel-- Ruth's mother lived in Italy and the two of them were there visiting. When they found out what was happening, they stayed extra time and didn't come home right away. And so they were not exposed to all this. We stayed at my aunt's for about two and a half weeks and then we were told that all of the furniture had to be moved out of the house in Speyer. So my mother made arrangements with a moving company to do that. And then about five days later, they told us, you can now come back. So all the furniture had to be brought back. So my mother went back to Speyer alone. I stayed with my grandmother, my aunt, and my cousin and we-- she made sure that everything was back in order. And then my mother-- my grandmother and I went by train, went back home. Meanwhile, we got letters regularly from my father, who asked for money so that he could buy things at Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he was at Dachau. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was at Dachau. So was my other uncle. And we started sending doctor's reports in indicating that he had a heart condition. And in early December, the 12th of December, all of a sudden one morning, early-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --the doorbell rang-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --and this scrawny, bearded man came in. It was my father. He hadn't had a haircut, he hadn't shaved, he hadn't showered probably in weeks. He was so emaciated it was unbelievable, but he was home. My Uncle Ernst did not get released until late in December, just before Christmas. And when he came home, he was in such horrible mental condition. He would scream at night, he wet the bed, all the after effects of the internment. They would tell us that for sometimes they would stand five, six hours in snow and rain out at attention because one of the prisoners was missing and if anybody fell over, they would just leave them lying there. And a lot of people didn't make it. But this was the gentle Dachau of the early days. It got worse afterwards. My Aunt Ruth and my cousin in Italy came home just before my uncle came home in December. And because they had been in an apartment near the railroad station, they moved in with us because it was safer. And so they were there when he came home. And then the seven of us lived together until we left Germany. The main focus then was twofold. Number one, my uncle and my father went to Mannheim every day to learn to become barbers because they felt they needed a trade if they went to another country and this was a trade that they could easily learn. The other thing was get papers to get out. By now, it was 1939. It was very difficult. We finally got papers in the beginning of '39. We went to Stuttgart and got-- had our examinations. And the visa was issued in April of '39 and it was good for three months. And we tried desperately-- no, four months, it was good for four months-- and we tried to get out desperately, but transportation was not to be had, number one. It had to be paid for from outside Germany. We couldn't pay for our own transportation. All the ships were full because everybody was trying to get out desperately. My Uncle Julius, who was the driver originally, when he came back, he had papers waiting for him. So he-- but they were only visitor's-- visitor's permit. So he left immediately after he came home, went to the United States, sat in front of one of my grandmother's brother's offices for two months until the man would give him a visa-- an affidavit, rather-- went back to Germany, got his visa, and they got out in August of 1939, just before the war started. They left through Amsterdam just before the war started. So they got out. We couldn't get out. We tried through Italy to Genoa, through Portugal, through Holland and Belgium. We couldn't get passage. And finally, the war had already been declared. My father went to Berlin to the biggest travel agency in Europe, which was [? Hansa, ?] and tried to get tickets to-- through-- on the Orient Express to go to Japan. And they said they had one ticket left and my father said, I will not go alone. My mother insisted, go. We can come afterwards, but you get out. He would not do it. And this is part of that personality thing that I mentioned before that I criticize him for. It's the only thing I can criticize him for. Anyway, he came back from Berlin without a ticket and there we sat. In the meanwhile, on September 1st, early in the morning, big ruckus on the streets. And I might add I was just reading my diary that I wrote later. Three days before, a man from the government delivered to us ration cards with an open date. And he told us at the time that he had those in his possession already since July of 1939. It is not something that Hitler wasn't planning. Anyway, on the 1st of September early in the morning, the ruckus on the streets was people driving away from the frontiers. They evacuated everybody from the Western front, from the Siegfried line area. And these people had little yellow rectangular signs on their cars. They had priority in everything. They were being put up in homes and the right direction was every home has to put up one family. So when we are offered a room, they [INAUDIBLE] the Jews. And-- but the schools were emptied and these people were living in the schools and they were living in any public building where they could put up cots. And then after a few days, they were moved further into Germany, further into the central part of Germany, where essentially, a lot of them remained the rest of their lives. They never went back. Later that day, Hitler made his famous speech that he had decided to invade Poland because of the Danzig perimeter, which was a road through Poland to get to Danzig, which was a German city. And he wanted the Danzig free, and as a result, he justified going into Poland and attacking Poland. He didn't care whether the English would do anything or the French would do anything. They can do anything they wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: We essentially remained where we were. Restrictions became very difficult to live with. For instance, you could not use a radio with anything but German stations. They would have sound trucks going down the streets which were essentially direction-finding equipment and they would monitor what radios were being listened to. And if you were listening to a foreign station, they would pick it up, come to the house, take your radio away. Also, they came and they confiscated all the silver, all the gold, all the valuables in the house. This was already between November '38 and September '39. And we could no longer travel without a permit. All of our passports were taken away if we had them, and my mother had one because we were in Switzerland the year before. All the passports were confiscated. You couldn't travel. You-- the men could no longer go by car to Mannheim, they had to go by train. They had to get a permit to go there every day and learn their barbering. In the meanwhile, trying to get visas was virtually impossible because there was nowhere to get / my father was still trying to get out through Genoa because Italy was in the war, but there was still a way to get out and America was neutral at that time. So the American consulate was still there. But-- because America had not declared a war. But it was totally impossible to get out, so we sat. And it became very nerve-racking to have these seven adults-- no five adults and the two children-- sitting there, nothing to do. Also, since the synagogue burned down, they had hired a hall near the synagogue where us children would go every day for schooling, schooling in quotation marks. It would be mothers who knew a little bit more than others who would teach us. And then once a week, a rabbi from another town would come and give us religious training. This was five days a week. And by this time, there were one two, three, four, five, six, maybe 10 children left in town.

speaker: interviewee
text: This continued through 1939 and it continued on until we left. The school was situated such a way that every day we had to walk past our burned out synagogue. And this was-- made t doubly hard. But anyway. When did you leave Speyer? How did that come about? All right. On November-- October 22nd, 1940, the war had been going on for a year. Holland had been invaded, France had been conquered. Early in the morning, about four o'clock in the morning, the doorbell rang, someone banging on the doors, open up, open up and once. My cousin and I were sleeping in my room, which had been our den before. We jumped out of bed and when we got to the hallway, there were four SS standing there fully armed, bayonets mounted-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --taking roll of who was in the house. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They then ordered all of us to be ready by eight o'clock, to pack, take three days' provisions with us, and take clothes as needed. My grandparents were in rather precarious health, and my mother and everybody else appealed that they wouldn't have to go. Later that morning, a supervisor came who was the [INAUDIBLE], the mayor of the town, and he guaranteed that my grandparents would not have to go and that they would be under his protective custody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the pressure was off a little bit. Cooking things to take was a little bit difficult at four or five o'clock in the morning, you didn't expect it. One thing that we always did was my grandmother and my mother always preserved eggs. This is a method of taking fresh eggs in the spring when they're cheap and putting them in water with a calcium solution, and that keeps the eggs fresh and they were used mostly for cooking. They wouldn't have-- in cooking, you wouldn't notice that little slightly off taste. So my mother started cooking off boiled-- hard boiled eggs, probably three dozen. And I remember we had a little suitcase that was my little children's suitcase that she filled with hard boiled eggs. So then a little bit later in the morning, probably about six, she said, we have no-- no bread and several other things. So they permitted me and my cousin to go to the bakery and pick up bread and to the grocery store and pick up whatever things we needed. The two of us were marched over there with two SS men at our side, down the street. The feeling that that gave you, walking past all these people that have known you for years under guard of two SS men as if you were a criminal, was not to be believed. We went to the bakery and the girl who knew me very well said, what's happening? I said, I don't know. I couldn't say anything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told her what we needed and she says, that's enough food for a week. I said, that's what it's supposed to be. And then we went to the grocery store next door and bought whatever it was, salami, that they had and other things and then came back home and finished packing. Around eight o'clock, a bus pulled up and we were still not told what was happening. We were put in the bus with all the other Jews from town and we were taken to [INAUDIBLE], which was in western [? Faltz. ?] It's near the French border. When we got there, we were let out at a big like a fairgrounds with a big hall. And there, we saw all these people from all over the area, from Bavaria as well as from the [? Faltz, ?] in this one place. And the rumor was that we were being sent to France, but nobody was sure. And we were told to just stay there, relax, wait for orders. They had food there, they had sandwiches there, they had water there, they had drinks there. So we could eat. But you couldn't leave the building. It was totally surrounded by SS. At one point, we got to talking to one of them later in the day and he was complaining he hadn't had any sleep in over 24 hours. We didn't really feel sorry for him, but we realized that this was an operation that had started long before we were woken up. Anyway, we stayed there overnight. Next morning, we were loaded in trains. These were regular passenger trains. And they started leaving one at a time. At one point, we were stopped on a railroad terminal and another train was added to ours and we found out that it was people from Wurms and Mainz that were on another train that was added to our train. And then the train started to move out into the occupied French area, so we [? knew were in ?] France. Oh, we found out earlier that we were going to France because they changed our German money, what we had. We were allowed to take 100 marks with us. They changed the 100 marks into French money, 100 marks per person. And then we were on this train going through all these towns. Every time the train stopped, the guards were out. You could not get off the train for any reason. Then on the second day, we woke up in the morning and there were no guards and we realized that we were in the unoccupied area of France, also known as Vichy France. And the train kept going. It was not considered a priority train, so every time a freight train came along, it was sidetracked to let the freight through. So it was a very slow progress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the train end up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wound up after three days, on the 25th of October, in a town called [INAUDIBLE] in southern France. We were unloaded into trucks and taken to Camp Gurs, which was located high in the Pyrenees and had been a camp for interning refugees from the Spanish Civil War and was now converted to hold Jews. The camp was divided into various sectors called [INAUDIBLE], some for men, some for women. The boys who were under 16 were left with their mothers. The others had to go with their fathers. We were divided up and taken to these different areas. We wound up in [INAUDIBLE] Barracks 24. They had no mattresses, nothing in the barracks. The barracks were designed for about 60 people, some of them had as many as 80 in them. After a while, they brought mattresses for the children and for the very old. And then a couple of days later, everybody got mattresses. The mattresses were nothing more than sacks filled with straw. And the first thing that everybody told us we had to have is sabot, which are galoshes, rubber galoshes. The area gets about 300 days of rain a year. It was nothing but a field of mud. And the mud would get so deep from the rain that you would walk to the toilet and lose your shoes in the mud because the suction from the water would just suck the shoes and the galoshes just right off your feet. And then you would have nothing but mud. And many a times, we would come back to the barracks with a handful of mud and our shoes in our hands and walking barefoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now and go to the next tape.
